title: AgeTech Concierge — AI care navigator for families with aging parents
tagline: The invisible layer that coordinates elder care so families don't burn out.
problem: When a parent turns 75+, managing their care becomes a second full-time job for adult children. Appointments, medications, insurance claims, home safety assessments, and social isolation all fall on family members with no coordination layer between them. There are hundreds of point solutions — telehealth apps, pill dispensers, fall sensors — but no orchestration layer that ties it all together.
solution: An AI-powered care concierge that acts as the command center for a senior's care. It ingests data from wearables, medical records, and family inputs, then proactively surfaces tasks: 'Your mum's next cardiology appointment is in 3 days — do you want me to arrange transport?' It handles insurance paperwork, medication reminders, and connects the family via a shared dashboard. Think Notion meets a geriatric care manager.
business_model: B2C subscription at $79/month per family. B2B2C via employer benefits (30M+ US adults are caregivers who lose productivity — employers pay for this). Enterprise tier for assisted living facilities to offer residents' families as a premium add-on.
why_now: 1 in 6 people globally will be 60+ by 2030. Less than 1% of VC goes to aging-focused startups. AI agent coordination tools are now mature enough to handle real workflow orchestration across health, logistics, and finance APIs.
moat: Data flywheel — the more care events it handles, the more predictive it becomes. Switching cost is high once a family's health history, insurance, and care network is inside the platform.
go_to_market: Partner with employer HR platforms (Workday, Rippling) to offer as a caregiving benefit. Target the 53 million unpaid caregivers in the US. Content marketing via guilt-free caregiving communities.
risks: Healthcare data regulation (HIPAA). Fragmented provider integrations. Requires trust in a sensitive domain — brand and tone are critical.
similar_companies: Honor (home care staffing), Papa (companion care), Homethrive (employer benefit), Wellthy (care concierge — closest comp, no AI layer)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with healthcare, family caregiving, or enterprise HR benefits experience.

title: SubPay — The payment operating system for construction subcontractors
tagline: Turn 56-day waits into 48-hour payments without factoring fees.
market_size: $2.2 trillion US construction industry; $280B drained annually by payment delays
problem: Subcontractors in construction are the industry's invisible bankers. They complete work, pay for materials and labor upfront, then wait an average of 56 days to get paid — sometimes 90 to 120 days. The 2025 National Subcontractor Market Report found that 77% of subs cover material costs out of pocket while waiting, 43% lack working capital for unexpected expenses, and 1 in 3 draws from personal savings to cover cash deficits. The root cause is not just slow GCs — it is that pay application submission, approval tracking, lien waiver exchange, and financing are all handled in disconnected tools or by hand, which creates compounding delays at every step.
solution: SubPay is a mobile-first platform that gives subcontractors one place to submit pay applications correctly the first time, track approval status in real time, exchange lien waivers digitally, and unlock early payment on approved invoices instantly. When a pay app is approved by the GC, the sub can draw 90% of the invoice value within 24 hours at a transparent flat fee — no traditional factoring, no relationship disclosure to the GC. The platform integrates with Procore and Buildertrend on the GC side to pull approval signals automatically, eliminating the chase-and-follow-up cycle that adds weeks to payment timelines.
business_model: SaaS subscription at $99/month per subcontractor company for the pay application and compliance layer. Early payment fee of 1.5-2.5% per 30-day term on drawn invoices — revenue scales with the subcontractor's invoice volume. Target average invoice size of $45K means each draw generates $675-$1,125 in fee revenue. Enterprise tier for trade associations and specialty contractors managing multiple crews at $299/month.
why_now: Mechanic's lien filings surged 141% in 2023 as payment conditions worsened — subcontractors are desperate for alternatives. GC platforms like Procore and Buildertrend now offer open APIs, making payment status data accessible programmatically for the first time. The embedded finance infrastructure (Banking-as-a-Service rails) has matured to where a small team can offer instant payouts without a banking license. Billd raised capital proving the demand exists, but left the workflow layer entirely unsolved.
moat: Network effects as GCs are onboarded — the more GCs that integrate, the faster approval signals flow and the lower the early payment risk. Proprietary payment history data on GC behaviour creates a unique credit model for subcontractors that traditional lenders cannot replicate. Switching cost compounds as compliance documents, lien waiver history, and pay app records accumulate on the platform.
go_to_market: Launch with a single trade vertical — electrical subcontractors — in one metro market (Dallas or Phoenix, both high-growth construction markets). Partner with NECA (National Electrical Contractors Association) and local trade chapters for distribution. Offer the compliance layer free for 90 days to drive adoption, then monetise on early payment draws. Referral program where subs who bring their GC onto the integration earn a rate discount.
risks: Lending license requirements vary by state and may require partnerships with bank sponsors. GC platform integrations depend on API access that could be restricted. Credit risk on early payment draws if GC does not ultimately pay. Competition from Procore and Buildertrend adding native payment features.
similar_companies: Billd (material financing for subs — does not solve the pay app workflow layer), Constrafor (GC-side procurement and compliance), Levelset (lien management only, acquired by Procore 2022), Payra (GC-side AR automation — opposite side of the relationship)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with construction industry operations experience combined with fintech or lending background. A technical co-founder familiar with financial APIs and BaaS infrastructure is essential.

title: SupplierCarbon — Scope 3 compliance tool built for suppliers, not buyers
tagline: Stop losing enterprise contracts because you can't answer a carbon data request.
market_size: $2.5 billion Scope 3 supplier data platforms market in 2024, growing to $8.7B by 2033 at 15% CAGR
problem: In 2025, over 70,000 mid-market suppliers received formal requests from enterprise customers to disclose their carbon emissions data via CDP — and 64% did not respond. The reason is not indifference: it is that every existing tool (Watershed, Persefoni, Sweep, EcoVadis) is priced for the Fortune 500 buyer at $180,000-$280,000 per year, leaving the supplier with nothing affordable or purpose-built. Meanwhile, California SB 253 requires Scope 3 data collection to begin in 2026 for 2027 reporting, and CSRD is pulling European supply chain disclosure obligations down to SME suppliers. A mid-market manufacturer that fails to respond to an enterprise customer's CDP or CSRD data request increasingly risks losing the contract — yet the average sustainability consultant charges $15,000-$50,000 just to file a single report.
solution: SupplierCarbon is a $149/month SaaS platform built specifically for the supplier side of Scope 3. It guides a company through measuring its own Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions (which become the Scope 3 data their customers need), packages the output in every required format — CDP questionnaire, CSRD ESRS data points, custom buyer portals — and stores it so the same data can be reused across multiple customer requests with one click. When a new data request arrives from a customer, the supplier logs in, selects the requesting customer's framework, reviews the pre-populated data, and submits in under 20 minutes. The platform also benchmarks the supplier's emissions against industry peers, identifies the top three reduction opportunities, and generates a plain-English summary the supplier can share proactively with their sales team to turn compliance into a competitive advantage.
business_model: SaaS subscription at $149/month (billed annually at $1,490) for companies with up to 500 employees. Mid-tier at $299/month for 500-2,000 employees. One-time data verification add-on at $499 for third-party limited assurance — required for California SB 253 and increasingly demanded by enterprise buyers. Distribution partnership revenue share with procurement platforms and trade associations who embed SupplierCarbon as a compliance module for their supplier networks.
why_now: California SB 253 mandates Scope 3 data collection starting in 2026 for reporting in 2027, creating a hard compliance deadline for thousands of US suppliers. In 2025, 70,000+ suppliers received CDP data requests — a 3x increase from five years prior — and only 36% responded, signalling massive unmet demand. The EU Omnibus directive in February 2026 narrowed CSRD scope but did not remove the obligation for large companies, who continue to push requirements down to their supply chains. Enterprise procurement teams are now explicitly scoring supplier responsiveness on sustainability data as a vendor qualification criterion.
moat: Reuse flywheel — every hour a supplier invests in entering their emissions data makes the platform stickier, as that data becomes reusable across all future customer requests. Multi-framework output means a supplier who sets up once can serve CDP, CSRD, ISSB, and custom buyer portals without re-entering data. Supplier network data creates an anonymised benchmarking database that becomes more accurate and valuable as the customer base grows — a defensible asset no newcomer can replicate quickly.
go_to_market: Target mid-market US manufacturers in California first — they face the earliest hard deadline under SB 253. Partner with the National Association of Manufacturers (NAM) and California Manufacturers and Technology Association (CMTA) to reach members en masse. Offer a free Scope 1 and 2 emissions baseline calculator as a lead magnet — users who complete it see their gap versus what their customers will request, creating natural conversion. Sales motion: identify suppliers who received a CDP request in 2025 but did not respond (CDP publishes this data publicly) and reach out directly with a 14-day free trial.
risks: Regulatory uncertainty — the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard is being revised with a public draft expected late 2026, which could require platform updates. Enterprise buyers may build supplier-facing portals themselves, reducing the need for an independent tool. Scope of emissions calculation requires sector-specific emission factors that must be kept current. Competing on price with free-tier offerings from incumbent platforms like EcoVadis.
similar_companies: Watershed (enterprise buyer-side platform, $180K+/year — no supplier-focused product), Persefoni (enterprise carbon accounting, not supplier-optimised), Ecochain (lifecycle assessment tool for product carbon footprints — adjacent but more technical), Hedgehog Carbon (small UK startup serving European suppliers — no US market presence or multi-framework output)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with sustainability consulting, enterprise SaaS sales, or supply chain management backgrounds. Deep familiarity with CDP questionnaire structure and GHG Protocol methodology is a meaningful advantage. A regulatory-literate technical co-founder who can build framework output adapters quickly is essential as the standard landscape shifts.

title: PathwayOS — The missing operating system for Career and Technical Education directors
tagline: CTE programs serve 11 million students with no purpose-built software to run them.
market_size: $1.4 billion in annual federal Perkins V funding; 11.2 million students enrolled across 30,000+ US CTE programs
problem: Career and Technical Education enrollment surged 10% in a single year — from 7.8 million to 8.6 million students between 2022-23 and 2023-24, and now exceeds 11.2 million nationwide. CTE directors are responsible for managing industry partnerships, tracking student credential attainment, demonstrating Perkins V federal funding compliance, and connecting graduating students with employers — all while running programs across automotive, healthcare, construction, IT, and culinary trades simultaneously. Yet 40% of CTE programs report struggling to maintain employer partnerships, 66% say students lack awareness of available pathways, and directors universally manage these complex operations through a combination of spreadsheets, generic school information systems built for academic subjects, and manual email chains. The EdTech market has produced hundreds of tools for academic instruction but almost nothing built specifically for the operational reality of a CTE program director.
solution: PathwayOS is a purpose-built SaaS platform for CTE program directors that unifies three critical workflows in one place. First, an employer partnership CRM that tracks every industry partner relationship — MOU status, work-based learning placements, guest speaker history, and advisory board participation — replacing the spreadsheets that currently fall apart when a director changes roles. Second, a student outcome tracker that follows each student from enrollment through credential attainment to employment, generating the Perkins V Consolidated Annual Report data automatically rather than requiring hundreds of manual hours at year-end. Third, a student-facing career pathway explorer that lets students browse program options, see local employer partners, and apply for work-based learning placements — addressing the awareness gap that 66% of CTE directors cite as their top challenge. The platform is district-licensed, meaning the data persists across staff turnover and scales across multiple program areas without reconfiguration.
business_model: District SaaS license at $8,000-$18,000 per year depending on number of CTE program pathways and student enrollment. This positions PathwayOS squarely within Perkins V local allocation spending — districts receive formula-based Perkins funding specifically to improve program quality, and software that automates compliance reporting is a direct and defensible use of these funds. State-level licenses for state CTE directors who want aggregated outcome data across all districts. Professional services revenue from Perkins V state plan alignment consulting for districts transitioning from manual reporting.
why_now: CTE enrollment has grown 10% year-over-year as employers and policymakers push vocational pathways as an alternative to four-year degrees. The Trump administration's 2026 federal budget proposes consolidating education programs and increasing state and local control — meaning districts that can demonstrate outcomes with data will protect their funding, while those that cannot may see cuts. Holon IQ's 2026 Global Education Outlook confirms workforce training attracted the most M&A activity in 2025, signalling rising investor and acquirer interest in the space. No purpose-built CTE operations platform exists at scale — the closest competitors are generic school administration tools that require heavy customisation.
moat: Institutional switching cost — once a district's employer partnerships, student outcome history, and Perkins compliance records live in PathwayOS, migration risk is extremely high, especially given staff turnover. Network effect within states: when multiple districts in a state use PathwayOS, the state CTE director gains an aggregated view that creates pressure for non-adopting districts to join. Perkins V reporting automation becomes more accurate over time as the platform learns district-specific credential and outcome mapping.
go_to_market: Launch with a single state — target Texas or California, both of which have large CTE enrollments and strong state-level CTE advocacy organisations. Partner with Advance CTE (the national membership organisation for state CTE directors) and the Association for Career and Technical Education (ACTE) to reach program directors. Offer a free Perkins V CAR report generator as a standalone tool — any director who uses it to save 200+ hours of year-end reporting work becomes a natural conversion target for the full platform. Attend SkillsUSA and ACTE National Policy Seminar to build relationships with early adopters.
risks: School district sales cycles are long — typically 6-18 months with procurement committee review. Perkins V funding allocation rules vary by state and may restrict how funds can be used for software. Staff turnover at the director level is high, meaning champions may leave mid-sale. Generic SIS vendors (PowerSchool, Infinite Campus) could add CTE-specific modules to their existing district relationships.
similar_companies: Xello (career exploration platform for K-12 — student-facing only, no director operations layer), YouScience (aptitude and career matching — assessment tool, not program management), Naviance (college and career readiness — higher ed focused, not CTE operations), Kuder (career planning platform — no employer partnership or Perkins compliance layer)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders who have worked inside a school district, state education agency, or CTE advocacy organisation and understand the Perkins V compliance burden firsthand. A technical co-founder who can build a clean, non-intimidating interface for non-technical program directors is essential — this is not a product that can afford enterprise UX complexity.

title: CreatorLedger — The financial operating system built for the creator economy
tagline: Know your real take-home pay after every platform cut, tax, and expense.
market_size: $260 billion creator economy in 2026; 207 million active creators globally with $31B spent on creator tools
problem: The creator economy reached $260 billion in 2026, yet 7 in 10 creators say their biggest concern is finances, and a quarter identify taxes as their primary source of anxiety. The problem is systemic: platforms like YouTube, TikTok, Patreon, Substack, and brand deal networks each pay out through different cycles, different fee structures, and different tax withholding rules — and none of them connect to each other. A creator earning $8,000 in a month might receive six separate deposits across three platforms, pay up to 30% in combined platform fees, owe GST or sales tax in multiple jurisdictions, and have no real-time view of what they actually earned. National Accounts in Australia confirmed in January 2026 that creators routinely discover their $5,000 payout becomes $2,500-$3,000 after fees and taxes are accounted for — but only at year-end. Existing tools like QuickBooks and Xero are built for product-based or service businesses with fixed income streams, not for the complex multi-platform, multi-currency, irregular income reality of a working creator. The only creator-specific options are expensive human bookkeeping services starting at $160/month, priced out of reach for the 71% of creators earning under $30,000 annually.
solution: CreatorLedger is a self-serve financial dashboard that connects directly to a creator's monetisation platforms — YouTube Studio, TikTok Creator Marketplace, Patreon, Substack, OnlyFans, Twitch, Shopify, and brand deal inbox — and in real time calculates their actual net income after platform revenue splits, estimated tax obligations, and logged business expenses. The core product is the Net Pay dashboard: at any moment a creator can see gross platform payouts this month, minus fees and splits, minus tax provisions, minus expenses, equals real take-home. Built-in quarterly tax estimation pre-fills BAS statements for Australian users or estimated tax vouchers for US users. An expense tracker that understands creator-specific categories — equipment, editing software, travel for content, props, contractor editors — with one-tap categorisation from a mobile camera. For creators with brand deals, a lightweight CRM logs deliverables, payment terms, and due dates so nothing falls through. The product is priced at $19/month, positioning it below a single coffee a day and well below the $160+/month human bookkeeping alternatives.
business_model: Consumer SaaS at $19/month (or $190/year for annual plan). Upsell to $49/month Creator Pro for multi-entity support, contractor payroll, and state/multi-jurisdiction tax filing. Affiliate referral program with accounting firms who handle year-end tax lodgements for creators who want human review — revenue share on referrals. Long-term: embedded banking and creator-specific financial products (e.g. income smoothing savings product that auto-saves tax provisions from every payout, drawing on the unique income pattern data the platform holds).
why_now: The creator economy is now a mainstream career path, not a side hustle. In Australia alone, 43% of the population earned side hustle income in the past year (Intuit QuickBooks, 2026) and 66% say they are likely to use AI tools to launch or formalise a business. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act in the US changed 1099-K reporting thresholds in 2026, creating new tax obligations that catch creators by surprise. Platform API access has matured — YouTube, TikTok, Patreon, and Stripe all publish data APIs that make automated income aggregation technically viable for the first time without manual CSV imports. The infrastructure for embedded tax estimation (e.g. Stripe Tax, open banking) now makes it possible to build this as a sub-$20/month product rather than a high-cost human service.
moat: Financial data lock-in — once a creator has 12 months of income history, expense records, and tax provisions in CreatorLedger, switching to a blank-slate tool carries real cost. Platform connection breadth becomes a moat: each new platform integration requires engineering investment that deepens the gap versus generic accounting tools. Creator-specific expense category models and tax rules (which differ from standard small business rules around equipment, travel, and home office) become more accurate as the dataset grows, creating a knowledge moat that generic tools cannot match without creator-specific training data.
go_to_market: Launch in Australia first — smaller, focused market with clear GST compliance pain and a government-backed push toward creator economy formalisation. Partner with creator communities and Discord servers (e.g. Creator Economy Australia, NZ Creator Collective) for beta access. Build a free public tool — a Creator Tax Estimator that asks five questions and outputs an estimated annual tax bill — as a viral lead magnet. Target the mid-tier creator segment: 10,000-500,000 followers, earning $1,000-$10,000/month, not yet able to afford a dedicated accountant. Influencer-led growth via honest finance content creators (a fast-growing subgenre) who become both users and distributors.
risks: Platform API access can be revoked or restricted — YouTube and TikTok have both tightened data access in the past. Tax rule complexity varies significantly by country and jurisdiction, limiting international expansion speed. Consumer fintech has a high churn rate if the product does not deliver immediate clear value. Xero or QuickBooks could release a creator-specific mode with their existing distribution advantage.
similar_companies: CoCountant (human bookkeeping for creators, $160/month+ — not self-serve software), Honeybook (business management for creative freelancers — not creator-platform connected), Bonsai (freelancer contracts and invoicing — no platform income aggregation), Karat Financial (creator-specific banking and credit card — financial products only, no tax or income visibility layer)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders who have been creators themselves or who have worked in creator monetisation, tax technology, or open banking. Understanding of the specific tax treatment of creator income in at least one jurisdiction (Australia or US) is essential to build the tax estimation layer correctly from day one. A product-led growth background is important — this is a self-serve consumer product that must deliver immediate value on signup.

title: ApprenticeOS — The workplace management platform for Australian apprentice employers
tagline: 45% of apprentices drop out. SME employers have no tools to stop it.
market_size: $2.4 billion in Australian government apprenticeship subsidies annually; 365,000+ active apprentices across 85% SME employers
problem: Australia has 365,000 active apprentices, and the government pumps $2.4 billion annually into employer subsidies to grow that number — yet only 54.8% of apprentices who start actually complete their training. The single largest driver of non-completion is not the apprentice: it is the workplace. Over 70% of dropouts cite employment-related issues — poor supervisor support, no structured progression, unclear skill milestones, and a mismatch between what the training organisation expects and what the employer tracks. SMEs employ over 60% of all Australian apprentices, and a 2023 ASQA audit found that 20% of SME employers offering apprenticeships failed to meet acceptable training standards — not from malice, but because they have no dedicated tools. The host employer's training obligation is tracked via paper logbooks, Word documents, and sporadic supervisor conversations. When a supervisor leaves, the entire training record goes with them. There is $2.4B flowing into a system where the critical employer-side infrastructure is a manila folder.
solution: ApprenticeOS is a mobile-first platform that gives small trade businesses a structured, digital workspace for managing their apprentices. A supervisor opens the app, sees each apprentice's current competency unit, and marks skills off as they are demonstrated on the job — replacing paper logbooks with time-stamped, geotagged digital sign-offs that satisfy ASQA requirements. Automated milestone alerts notify supervisors when an apprentice is approaching a progression point or has gone three weeks without a logged skill sign-off — the single most predictive early warning signal for dropout risk. The platform generates the documentation required for quarterly training reviews with the Registered Training Organisation (RTO), eliminating the two-to-three hours of paperwork that most supervisors currently do (or skip). For the apprentice, a companion app shows their progress toward trade completion, their upcoming assessments, and a direct message channel with their supervisor — addressing the isolation and lack of feedback that NCVER identifies as key dropout triggers. The platform integrates with the major Australian RTOs' student management systems so competency data flows both ways without double entry.
business_model: SaaS subscription at $49/month per active apprentice position for the employer, billed monthly with no lock-in — aligning cost directly with the government subsidy the employer receives (up to $5,000 per apprentice per year under the Australian Apprenticeships Incentive System, making $49/month a trivially justifiable line item). Group employer license for labour hire firms and Group Training Organisations (GTOs) managing large cohorts at negotiated per-seat rates. RTO partnership channel: RTOs who recommend ApprenticeOS to their host employers receive a revenue share and branded reporting dashboard showing completion rates across their entire employer network. Government tender pathway: state training departments are actively seeking tools to improve completion rates and would pay per-apprentice licensing for state-wide adoption.
why_now: The Australian government committed $2.4 billion specifically to boost apprenticeship commencements and completions after the NCVER flagged structural weaknesses in early 2025. The Digital Apprenticeships initiative is actively funded and seeking scalable employer-facing tools. The stagnant 54.8% completion rate has become a political problem — every percentage point improvement is worth hundreds of millions in economic output. RTOs are under increasing ASQA scrutiny for employer compliance, creating institutional demand for tools that generate audit-ready documentation. The move to digital training records is now mandated across most Australian states, removing the last reason for employers to resist moving off paper.
moat: Completion record data — ApprenticeOS accumulates the most granular dataset in Australia on what on-the-job training practices correlate with apprenticeship completion, creating a predictive dropout risk model no competitor can replicate without the same longitudinal data. RTO integration network effects: each RTO that integrates creates a distribution channel pulling all their employer partners onto the platform. Switching cost is high because the digital sign-off and competency records accumulated over a four-year apprenticeship become the apprentice's verified portfolio of evidence — neither employer nor apprentice wants to lose or re-enter that record.
go_to_market: Partner with the five largest RTOs in the electrical and plumbing trades in NSW and Victoria — together they cover roughly 40,000 active apprentices and have a direct relationship with every host employer in their network. Offer a free 90-day trial to any employer referred by an RTO partner, removing procurement friction entirely. Attend Master Electricians Australia and Master Plumbers conferences to reach the trade employer associations that already have trusted relationships with small business owners. Build a free public tool — an Apprenticeship Completion Risk Checker — that asks employers five questions about their current practices and outputs a risk score with benchmarks, converting awareness into pipeline.
risks: Government policy changes to apprenticeship funding structures could shift the economic case for employers. RTO systems are highly fragmented with legacy technology, making integrations slow and expensive to build. Some employers may resist digital tracking if they perceive it as increasing ASQA audit exposure for current non-compliance. Competition from the national eSkilled platform (government-backed digital logbook pilot) if it gains traction and becomes mandated.
similar_companies: Wisenet (RTO student management system — manages the training provider side, not the employer side), aXcelerate (another RTO SMS — same limitation), Apprentice Now (US-based apprenticeship management, no Australian market presence or RTO integration), eSkilled (government-backed digital logbook pilot — narrow in scope, not a full employer workflow platform)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders with direct experience in the Australian VET sector — either running an RTO, working in workforce development, or as a trade employer who has navigated ASQA audits. Understanding of the Training Package competency unit structure is essential to build the skill sign-off layer correctly. A mobile-first product background is important — this product is used on worksites, not desks.

title: FarmPass — The guided succession platform for Australian farming families
tagline: Only 14% of Australian farmers have a succession plan. Help them fix that.
market_size: $800B+ in Australian agricultural assets; 85,000+ family farms with average owner age in mid-50s to early 60s requiring succession in the next decade
problem: Australia faces a looming farm succession crisis that Bloomberg called one of the biggest wealth transfer challenges in the country's history in February 2026. The average Australian farmer is in their mid-50s to early 60s, yet only 14% of dairy farmers have a finalised succession plan and only 19% of Australian family businesses overall have a documented plan in place (Grant Thornton 2025 Family Business Report). The consequences of poor planning are severe and well-documented: inter-generational family disputes lasting decades, forced property sales to pay out departing siblings, farms split into unviable parcels, and significant mental health consequences for farming families. The process of farm succession involves at least four distinct professional domains — legal (estate planning, trust structures), financial (tax minimisation, valuations, capital gains), operational (who takes over which roles, when), and family (how to have the hard conversations) — yet no tool coordinates them. Farming families navigate this once-in-a-generation process using a combination of generic legal templates, occasional accountant meetings, and avoidance. Victorian land transfer fees on properties above $3M more than doubled from 1 July 2026, making the cost of waiting even higher.
solution: FarmPass is a structured, multi-year digital platform that guides a farming family through every stage of succession from first conversation to completed legal transfer. At onboarding, the platform asks a structured set of questions about the farm — land parcels, entities, family members, debt, and intentions — and generates a personalised Succession Readiness Score with a clear gap analysis across the four domains: legal, financial, operational, and family. From there, FarmPass provides a milestone roadmap with time-estimates for each step, educational content matched to the family's specific situation, templates for the conversations that are hardest to start (non-farming siblings, retirement timing, management handover), and a shared workspace where the farming family and their advisers — accountant, solicitor, financial planner — can all see the current state and next actions. The platform does not replace professional advisers — it organises and prepares families so those professional conversations are far more productive and focused. For the 81% of farming families who currently have no plan at all, FarmPass provides the structure to start.
business_model: Family SaaS subscription at $49/month (or $490/year) — positioned as less than one hour of professional adviser time per month, and trivially small relative to the millions of dollars of assets and tax exposure at stake. Adviser network model: accountants, rural financial counsellors, and agribusiness solicitors who service farming clients can white-label FarmPass or refer clients and receive a revenue share, creating a B2B2C distribution channel through the same professionals already involved in farm finances. Sponsored by rural financial institutions: banks (NAB Agribusiness, Rabobank) and insurers serving agricultural clients could subsidise FarmPass subscriptions for their farming customers as a value-added retention service. Government grant eligibility: Rural Financial Counselling Service (RFCS) and state agricultural departments actively fund tools that improve farm business planning capability.
why_now: The farm succession crisis has moved from a slow-burn problem to an urgent one in 2026. Victorian land transfer fee increases from July 2026 significantly raise the cost of delayed planning. Bloomberg's February 2026 feature accelerated public awareness. The Rural Financial Counselling Service has flagged succession as its top priority issue nationally. Meanwhile, a cohort of farmers who expanded aggressively during the commodity boom of 2020-2023 now hold significantly more debt alongside significantly higher land valuations — a combination that makes unplanned succession more financially catastrophic than ever. Digital adoption among farmers has increased substantially post-pandemic, with the ABS reporting 78% of agricultural businesses now use cloud-based tools for some business function.
moat: Completion record and data lock-in — a family that has entered their land parcels, entity structures, family member details, and advisor contacts into FarmPass over two or three years will not abandon that record. The platform accumulates context that makes every professional engagement more efficient, creating switching cost. Network effects with advisers: an accountant or solicitor who uses FarmPass with ten farming families becomes an embedded distribution channel. Anonymised aggregate data on farm succession patterns across Australia creates a benchmarking capability that becomes more accurate and more valuable as the user base grows — no competitor can replicate without the same longitudinal dataset.
go_to_market: Launch through the Rural Financial Counselling Service (RFCS) network — over 150 counsellors across Australia who provide free financial counselling to farming families and who actively seek tools to help clients initiate succession conversations. Partner with NAB Agribusiness and Rabobank, both of which have stated publicly they are focused on farm succession as a systemic risk in their agricultural lending portfolios. Target state-based farmer membership organisations (VFF in Victoria, NSW Farmers, Grain Producers Australia) for co-branded distribution. Build a free public tool — a 10-minute Succession Readiness Quiz — that generates a score and gap report, converting awareness into email subscribers who become trial users.
risks: Farm succession planning is deeply emotionally charged — some families will resist a digital tool for what they perceive as a family matter. Professional adviser channel conflict if accountants or solicitors perceive the platform as reducing their billable hours. Australian state-by-state variation in trust law, land transfer rules, and stamp duty creates complexity in the legal guidance layer. Long sales cycle: the average time from first awareness to first payment for a tool like this may be 6-18 months given how long families defer succession decisions.
similar_companies: Maus (general business succession software for advisers — not farm-specific, no operational or family conversation layer), Agworld (farm operations management — tracks cropping and livestock decisions, succession planning blog post but no dedicated module), FarmLink (rural brokerage and advisory services — human-led, not digital), Grant Thornton / RSM Australia (produce farm succession reports and advisory services — high-cost human service, no self-serve software product)
founder_fit: Ideal for founders who grew up on a farm or have directly experienced farm succession in their own family — the emotional authenticity this brings to the product and the sales motion is irreplaceable. A background in rural financial counselling, agribusiness banking, or agricultural law is a strong advantage for building the structured content layer correctly. Product experience in SaaS tools for professional services (legal tech, wealth management) is valuable for navigating the adviser distribution channel.
